Total Time Taken: 2 hours and 37 minutes ## Bookmarks > But if the scope grows too broad, the story becomes clunky and unhelpful. Here we are better off embracing what the poet John Keats called “negative capability,” or what the Heath brothers called “forced prioritization”: appreciate the nuances of multiple perspectives, but don’t linger there too long. Think of how Shakespeare introduces characters without revealing their background and presents issues with no clear answer or solution. Because he recognizes that the world is unsettled and that a story will never be “complete,” the playwright allows readers to explore different angles and imagine how a story could come to any number of different conclusions. That’s why Portia is such a compelling character: through her inventive solution she defies expectations and slyly encourages the audience to do the same. > 2025-11-16


For example, an intense workout regimen could lead to an exercise addiction or a knee injury. If you go on a long-haul protein-heavy diet, you could develop a liver problem years later or encounter other problems that you can’t now predict. Does this mean you should abandon your weight-loss goal? Of course not. But you need to acknowledge that a limited criterion of good health (like losing some weight) doesn’t, can’t, and shouldn’t account for what is complex and, too often, unpredictable. Instead of fixating on and planning for one be-all and end-all target, find and explore different pathways that embrace complexity and seek health instead of pretending that good planning will lead to a perfect solution that will make the problem disappear 2025-11-16

title: The Divided Self
text:
Definitions

Schizoid - A person who is caught between the desire to be connected and the fear of losing himself if he connects

There is a chasm in relation with his world
There is a disruption of his relation with himself

Existentialism - What it means to exist as a human being and how personal freedom, individual responsibility, and deliberate choice are essential to the pursuit of self-discovery and the determination of life’s meaning
Phenomenology - How human beings experience the world
Existential Phenomenology - A characterisation of the nature of a person’s experience of his world and himself
Concretum - The wholeness of experience as a human, including all feelings and not just descriptive, POV is from the experience of the self.
Conditio sine qua non (Latin) - “A condition without which not” (a necessary condition)
Gestalt (German) - “Form, shape or pattern” The felt whole of an experience, structured and meaningful, not a loose collection of bits
Reification - Treating something non-material, living, dynamic (like a person, thought or relationship) as if it were a static, dead thing. “The person is no longer seen as being-in-the-world with hopes, fears, and struggles, but as a ‘case of schizophrenia’ or a set os symptoms. This is reification.”
Veritable - Genuine, true, authentic
Esse est percipi - “To be is to be perceived”
Imago - A deep internal image or representation of another person, often formed in early childhood, that continues to shape how we relate to others - and ourselves
Shutupness - The self’s last-ditch attempt to survive by sealing itself off — but in doing so, it forgets how to be. Eventually, even joy becomes unfeelable, even pain becomes unshareable, the person may no longer know how to tell if they’re alive.
Embodied - Having a sense of being flesh and blood and bones, of being biologically alive and real, he knows himself to be substantial. To the extent that he is throughly ‘in’ his body, he is likely to have a sense of personal continuity in time. He is implicated in bodily desire, and the gratifications and frustrations of the body. The individual thus has his starting point an experience of his body as a base from which he can be a person with other human beings.
Unembodied - The individual experiences his self as being more or less divorced or detached from the body. The body is felt more as one object among other objects in the world than as the core of the individual’s own being. Instead of being the core of his true self, the body is felt as the core of the false self, which a detached, disembodied, “inner”, “true” self looks on at with tenderness, amusement, or hatred as the case may be.
Pabulum - Intellectual or emotional nourishment that is bland, unchallenging, and often oversimplified, especially when its offered to someone who is a vulnerable or dependent state. Laing uses pabulum to critique the kind of simplistic, often condescending, intellectual or emotional “feeding” that prevents individuals from truly exploring and understanding their own complex realities, especially in the context of mental distress.
Sclerosis - A metaphorical representation of a process of hardening, rigidity, and loss of adaptive capacity or vitality within the individual’s psychological or relational life, often stemming from the pressures and anxieties of a divided self.
Eschewed - deliberately avoided, shunned, or abstained from
Congenial - agreeable, suitable, or naturally fitting, especially in terms of disposition, character or environment
Sentiment du reel - refers to the fundamental feeling of being alive, present, and connected to a shared reality
Contiguously - adjacent, touching, or in close proximity, either physically or conceptually, without necessarily being causally linked or inwardly connected
Onanism - could mean withdrawal of the penis in sexual intercourse so that ejaculation takes place outside the vagina.

Laing probably refers to existential failure to embody one’s potential and express one’s true being in the world, leading to a profound sense of unlived life and an absence of genuine fulfillment
Or the gratification or satisfaction of one’s own needs or desires:

Catatonic - Marked by prominent psychomotor disturbances. This means unusual and extreme patterns of movement and behavior
Hebephrenic - prominent disorganization of thought, speech, and behavior, along with a marked disturbance in emotional expression. This type often had an earlier onset, typically in adolescence

title: The Divided Self
text:
Key points

1. The Limitations of Language and Abstract Theory in Understanding Human Experience

Summary: Laing argues that specialized clinical or theoretical language, by abstracting and compartmentalizing human experience (e.g., “mind,” “body,” “ego”), inherently distorts and isolates the individual. This approach fails to capture the holistic and relational nature of human being, as we are inextricably linked to our world and others.
Original Points Combined:


“How can one demonstrate the general human relevance and significance of the patient’s condition if the words one has to use are specifically designed to isolate the circumscribe the meaning of the patient’s life to a particular clinical entity”


The inherent fallibility of any “theory that begins with man or a part of man abstracted from his relation with the other in the world. We all know from our personal experience that we can be ourselves only in and through our world and there is a sense in which ‘our’ world will die with us although ‘the’ world will go on without us.”


e.g. “Mind and Body”, “Psyche and Soma”, “Psychological and Physical”, “Personality”, “the Self”, “the Organism”, “the Ego”, “the Superego”, “the Id”


Reasoning for Combination: All three points speak to the same overarching critique: that the language and conceptual frameworks often used to describe human experience (especially in psychology/psychiatry) are inherently reductive, isolating, and fail to capture the holistic, relational reality of a person in their world. The examples directly illustrate the problematic nature of these abstract terms.



2. The Power of Initial Perception and its Implications

Summary: Our fundamental way of perceiving something—whether as a mere “organism” or a complex “person”—establishes the entire framework for how we subsequently relate to it. This initial categorization profoundly shapes our interactions and understanding.
Original Points Combined:


The initial way we see a thing determines all our subsequent dealings with it


One’s relationship to an organism is different from one’s relation to a person


Reasoning for Combination: These two points are highly interdependent. The second point (“organism vs. person”) serves as a prime example of the principle stated in the first (“initial way we see a thing determines all our subsequent dealings”). Combining them highlights this cause-and-effect relationship.



3. Sanity as a Social Construct: Power, Consensus, and Conformity

Summary: Laing argues that sanity is not an absolute state but a relational one, defined by the degree of shared reality or “conjunction” between individuals. If two people agree on reality, they deem each other sane. However, when disjunction occurs, the power to define who is “mad” often rests with the socially authorized side. This suggests psychiatry, by enforcing consensus, might be punishing deviation rather than curing objective madness.
Original Points Combined:


“Sanity or psychosis is tested by the degree of conjunction or disjunction between two persons where the one is sane by common consent”


If you and I agree on reality, we call each other sane. But if I disjoin from you, then you might call me mad


e.g. A person sees angels in the room, a psychiatrist doesn’t. The person is viewed to be hallucinating while the psychiatrist is viewed to be blind to higher realities. The disjunction is symmetrical but only one side is authorised by society to define the other


Therefore, if sanity is defined by consensus, then psychiatry merely enforces conformity, not truth. Psychiatry may not be curing madness but punishing deviance.


Reasoning for Combination: All these points directly contribute to Laing’s core argument about the socio-political nature of sanity. The examples and the concluding implication flow directly from the initial premise that sanity is defined by consensus.



4. Existential Anxieties of Ontological Insecurity
Summary: Individuals who lack a secure sense of self (ontological insecurity) experience profound anxieties, including the fear of engulfment (being consumed by others’ perspectives), implosion (internal collapse), and petrification (loss of vitality or becoming an object).
Original points combined:

Forms of anxiety encountered by the ontologically insecure person:

Engulfment

“You are arguing in order to have the pleasure of triumphing over me. At best you win an argument, at worst you lose an argument. I am arguing in order to preserve my existence.”

Implosion
Petrification




5. Paradoxical Defenses to Safeguard the Self
Summary: To preempt dreaded dangers, individuals may employ counterintuitive strategies that seem self-defeating but are designed to secretly preserve their autonomy. This involves actions like forfeiting overt control to covertly maintain it, or engaging in self-consumption to avoid being consumed by another.
Original points combined:

The dangers most dreaded can themselves be encompassed to forestall their actual occurrence.

To forgo one’s autonomy becomes the means of secretly safeguarding it
To understand oneself (engulf oneself) is a defense against the risk involved in being sucked into the whirlpool of another person’s way to comprehending oneself
To consume oneself by one’s own love prevents the possibility of being consumed by another




6. The Autonomy-Dependency Dilemma: Isolation vs. Merging

Summary
Individuals lacking a secure sense of autonomy cannot experience healthy separateness or relatedness.


Original Points Combined
This lack of autonomy means they feel their being is excessively bound to others, or vice versa, in a way that goes beyond normal human connection. Instead of genuine relatedness and attachment, they feel an ontological dependency on the other for their very existence. This leads to a constant oscillation between two equally unfeasible extremes: complete isolation or complete merging of identity, rather than a healthy balance of separateness and relatedness. This can manifest as withdrawing for months, feeling like they’re “dying inside,” only to seek brief “doses” of social interaction to avoid an “overdose.”



7. Case Studies in Ontological Insecurity: Seeking Identity and Avoiding Self

Summary
These cases illustrate how a lack of early relational support can lead to profound ontological insecurity, manifesting as either an inability to maintain a sense of self without external validation or the adoption of a “false self” to cope with unpredictable environments.


Original Points Combined
Case 1: Anxiety at Feeling Alone describes a patient whose parents were engrossed in each other, leaving her unnoticed. She grew up with a deep longing to be important to someone, never becoming self-sufficient. Her core issue was a lack of ontological autonomy; her sense of identity would “drain away” if she wasn’t in the actual presence of someone who knew her, or if she couldn’t evoke their presence.
Case 2: The Individual Seems to Be the Vehicle of the Personality That Is Not His Own features a patient whose parents were unpredictable in expressing affection or disapproval. To cope, she would become her mother’s perceived personality (e.g., hating everyone), which temporarily alleviated her fear. However, this maneuver left her with profound futility at not being herself and a hatred for the person she had been (her mother) and for her own “self-duplicity,” leading her to question if avoiding anxiety by not being herself was a worse “cure than her disease.”



8. The Embodied and Unembodied Self: David’s Schizoid Dilemma

Summary
David’s case exemplifies the schizoid split between a “true” (unembodied) self and a “personality” (false self). This individual seeks omnipotence, safety, and freedom by withdrawing their true self from genuine engagement with the world, leading to profound emptiness and an inability to experience true connection or meaning.


Original Points Combined
David grew up believing his “self” and “personality” were separate, striving “never to give himself away.” This central split meant he tried to achieve omnipotence by enclosing within himself, without genuine relationships, the very modes of relating that require others. The imagined advantages were safety for the true self, isolation and freedom, self-sufficiency, and control. However, this impossible project leads to persistent despair and a “haunting sense of futility.” The hidden, shut-up self, by disowning participation in the false self’s activities, lives “only mentally” and becomes increasingly impoverished, eventually feeling like a “vacuum.”
The world and other people are perceived as dangers, so the self seeks to be unembodied to transcend and thus be safe. This constant dread of being overwhelmed is ironically potentiated, yet the self “may at the same time long more than anything for participation in the world.” This longing is felt as a weakness, and giving in to it is dreaded, as participation threatens to obliterate the self’s “vacuum” or lead to engulfment.
The consequence is that the individual delegates all interactions to a system “which is not ‘him’,” resulting in an unreal experience of the world and a sense that all actions are “false, futile and meaningless.” The healthy relationship (Self/body) ↔︎ other becomes Self ↔︎ (body/other), precluding the self from direct engagement with real things and people.



9. The Schizoid Struggle with Friendship and Interdependence

Summary
The schizoid person’s life often founders on the fundamental question of self-sufficiency versus genuine need for others, making true friendship difficult as they question why two “complete” beings would desire anything from each other.


Original Points Combined
The discussion of the possibility of friendship gets stuck on a dilemma: if two beings are “not ‘wanting’ in anything,” why should they want anything from the other? This central issue is where the schizoid person’s life is liable to founder – the profound question of whether they are truly self-sufficient or if they genuinely “want” anything from another person.



10. Compulsive Caricature as Indirect Expression

Summary
James’s behavior exemplifies how individuals can indirectly express unresolved feelings, like aggression, by compulsively exaggerating and mimicking another’s irritating traits, thereby evoking from others the emotions they cannot directly confront in the original relationship.


Original Points Combined
James “took after” his father in compulsively asking guests if they’d had enough to eat and pressing them to have more, even when they were satisfied. What started as apparent generosity became a compulsive nuisance causing general embarrassment. In this, he “took up what he sensed were the aggressive implications in his father’s actions, and exposed these implications, through exaggerating them in his own adaptation, to general ridicule and anger.” He effectively evoked from others the feelings he had towards his father but couldn’t express directly, creating a “satirical comment on his father through the medium of a compulsive caricature of him.”



11. The Dual Role of Self-Consciousness for the Ontologically Insecure

Summary
For the ontologically insecure person, self-consciousness serves a contradictory purpose: it both confirms existence through being seen, yet simultaneously exposes them to profound danger by making them vulnerable to the gaze of others, often leading to defensive attempts to become “invisible.”


Original Points Combined
In the ontologically insecure person, self-consciousness plays a double role. On one hand, being aware of oneself and knowing that others are aware of them is a crucial means of assuring themselves of their own existence and the existence of others. This external validation anchors their fragile sense of being. However, in a perceived world full of danger, to be a “potentially seeable object” means being constantly exposed to vulnerability. Self-consciousness, in this context, becomes an apprehensive awareness of oneself as perpetually at risk simply by being visible to others. The natural defense against such a pervasive danger is to make oneself “invisible” in some manner, whether physically, emotionally, or socially.



12. The Dual Nature of Self-Consciousness
R.D. Laing proposes that self-consciousness is a complex phenomenon involving two distinct yet interconnected dimensions. Firstly, it encompasses an awareness of oneself by oneself—our private, internal sense of our thoughts, feelings, and existence, akin to introspection and self-reflection. Secondly, and equally crucial, it includes an awareness of oneself as an object of someone else’s observation. This refers to our perception of how we are seen, evaluated, and experienced by others, influencing our social behavior and self-presentation. For individuals experiencing ontological insecurity, Laing argues that both these dimensions become intensely amplified and often distorted, leading to a tormenting hyper-awareness of their inner states and a terrifying preoccupation with how they are perceived by the external world.


13. The Paradox of Self-Preservation
For individuals experiencing profound ontological insecurity, Laing identifies a paradoxical strategy: “the denial of being as a means of preserving being.” Faced with the terrifying prospect of their own annihilation or disintegration, they may retreat from authentic existence, disengaging from genuine relationships and even their own embodied self. By denying their “true” self and adopting a “false self” or withdrawing into an inner world, they attempt to create a secure, albeit isolated, haven. This defensive maneuver, however, often leads to a deeper sense of unreality and alienation, as the very act of denying their being ultimately prevents them from truly living and affirming their existence in the world.


14. The Desperate Search for Reality Through Pain
Laing highlights a tragic paradox in the struggle for reality: when the ordinary “sentiment du réel” is absent, some individuals may resort to extreme measures to simply feel something real. This can manifest as self-inflicted pain or deliberately courting terror. For instance, a schizophrenic woman might engage in acts like stubbing cigarettes on her hand or tearing out her hair, not for masochistic pleasure, but because these intense sensations offer a fleeting, tangible experience that contrasts with her pervasive feeling of unreality. Similarly, a schizoid person might seek out dangerous thrills or “kicks” in a desperate attempt to “scare some life into themselves,” hoping to jolt their system into recognizing its own existence. These acts underscore a profound alienation, where the ability to feel pain remains intact, but the fundamental sensation of being alive and real is agonizingly absent.


Original Text
A further attempt to experience real alive feelings may be made by subjecting oneself to intense pain or terror. Thus, one schizophrenic woman who was in the habit of stubbing out her cigarettes on the back of her hand, pressing her thumbs hard against her eyeballs, slowly tearing out her hair, etc., explained that she did such things in order to experience something “real’. It is most important to understand that this woman was not courting masochistic gratification; nor was she anaesthetic. Her sensations were not less intense than normal. She could feel everything except being alive and real. Minkowski reports that one of his patients set fire to her clothing for similar reasons. The cold schizoid person may go for kicks’ , court extreme thrills, push himself into ex-treme risks in order to ’scare some life into himself”.


15. The Desperate Search for Reality Through Pain
Laing highlights a tragic paradox in the struggle for reality: when the ordinary “sentiment du réel” is absent, some individuals may resort to extreme measures to simply feel something real. This can manifest as self-inflicted pain or deliberately courting terror. For instance, a schizophrenic woman might engage in acts like stubbing cigarettes on her hand or tearing out her hair, not for masochistic pleasure, but because these intense sensations offer a fleeting, tangible experience that contrasts with her pervasive feeling of unreality. Similarly, a schizoid person might seek out dangerous thrills or “kicks” in a desperate attempt to “scare some life into themselves,” hoping to jolt their system into recognizing its own existence. These acts underscore a profound alienation, where the ability to feel pain remains intact, but the fundamental sensation of being alive and real is agonizingly absent.


16. From Self-Annihilation to the Quest for “Nobody”
This passage describes a pivotal moment, or perhaps a re-conceptualization, in Rose’s journey, suggesting an arrest in her self-destructive trajectory. From an existential viewpoint, she ceased her systematic attempt to “murder herself,” recognizing that her life had become a relentless endeavor to obliterate her own identity and become “nobody.” She meticulously avoided any action, location, or relationship that could specifically define her as a real person. Instead of using actions to achieve real ends and thereby define herself, she sought to reduce herself to a “vanishing-point” by ensuring her acts had no real consequences, thus making them unreal themselves. This pervasive effort to dissociate from her own actions—even in work, friendships, gestures, and expressions—was her desperate means to achieve non-existence, feeling she had denied herself access to the “freshness and forgivingness of creation” that even the simplest of beings, like a girl in a film finding joy in clowns or a song, could experience.

title: The Divided Self
section: 17. The Self and False-self in a Schizophrenic
text:
17. The Self and False-self in a Schizophrenic
The divorce of the self from the body is both something which is painful to be borne, and which the sufferer desperately longs for someone to help mend, but it is also utilised as the basic means of defence. This in fact defines the essential dilemma. The self wishes to be wedded to and embedded in the body, yet is constantly afraid to lodge in the body for fear of there being subject to attacks and dangers which it cannot escape. Yet the self finds that though it is outside the body it cannot sustain the advantages that it might hope for in this position.
What happens in this scenario: 1. Its orientation is a primitive oral one, concerned with the dilemma of sustaining its aliveness, while being terrified to ‘take in’ anything. It becomes parched with thirst, and desolate. 2. It becomes charged with hatred of all that is there. The only way of destroying and of not destroying what is there may be felt to be to destroy itself. 3. The attempt to kill the self may be undertaken intentionally. It is partly defensive (‘If I’m dead, I can’t be killed’); partly an attempt to endorse the crushing sense of guilt that oppresses the individual (no sense of a right to be alive). 4. The ‘inner’ self becomes itself split, and loses its own identity and integrity. 5. It loses its own realness and direct access to realness outside itself. 6. The place of safety of the self becomes a prison. Its would be haven becomes a hell 1. It ceases even to have the safety of a solitary cell. Its own enclave becomes a torture chamber. The inner self is persecuted within this chamber by split concretised parts of itself or by its own phantoms which have become uncontrollable

Sanity is not an absolute state and is defined relationally

“Sanity or psychosis is tested by the degree of conjunction or disjunction between two persons where the one is sane by common consent”
If you and I agree on reality, we call each other sane. But if I disjoin from you, then you might call me mad
e.g. A person sees angels in the room, a psychiatrist doesn’t. The person is viewed to be hallucinating while the psychiatrist is viewed to be blind to higher realities. The disjunction is symmetrical but only one side is authorised by society to define the other
Therefore, if sanity is defined by consensus, then psychiatry merely enforces conformity, not truth. Psychiatry may not be curing madness but punishing deviance.

Forms of anxiety encountered by the ontologically insecure person:

Engulfment

“You are arguing in order to have the pleasure of triumphing over me. At best you win an argument, at worst you lose an argument. I am arguing in order to preserve my existence.”

Implosion
Petrification

The dangers most dreaded can themselves be encompassed to forestall their actual occurrence.

To forgo one’s autonomy becomes the means of secretly safeguarding it
To understand oneself (engulf oneself) is a defense against the risk involved in being sucked into the whirlpool of another person’s way to comprehending oneself
To consume oneself by one’s own love prevents the possibility of being consumed by another

If the individual does not feel himself to be autonomous, this means that he can experience neither his separateness from, or his relatedness to, the other in the usual way.

This lack of sense of autonomy implies that one feels one’s being to be bound up in the other, or that the other is bound up in oneself, in a sense that transgresses the actual possibilities within the structure of human relatedness.
It means that a feeling that one is in a position of ontological dependency on the other (i.e. dependent on the other for one’s very own being), is substituted for a sense of relatedness and attachment to him based on genuine mutuality.
Therefore, the polarity is between complete isolation or complete merging of identity rather than between separateness and relatedness. The individual oscillates perpetually, between the two extremes, each equally unfeasible.
Another patient maintained himself in isolated detachment from the world for months, but in doing this, he began to feel he was dying inside. He would emerge into social life for a brief foray in order to get a ‘dose’ of other people, but ’not an overdose
Case 1: Anxiety at feeling alone

No open neglect or hostility in her family. But her parents were always too engrossed in each other for either of them to take notice of her. She grew up wanting to fill this hole in her life but never succeeded in becoming self-sufficient or absorbed in her own world. Her longing was always to be important and significant to someone else.
The pivotal point around which all her life is centred is her lack of ontological autonomy. If she is not in the actual presence of another person who knows her, or if she cannot succeed in evoking the persons’s presence in his absence, her sense of her own identity drains away from her.

Case 2: The individual seems to be the vehicle of the personality that is not his own.

Her parents were, or she felt they were, completely unpredictable and unreliable in their expression of love or hatred, approval or disapproval.
Like her mother (or what her mother is probably assumed to be like), she hated everyone. In fact, life was a misery to her by the fact that she could never be herself but was always being her mother.
She knew that if she worked herself up into becoming her mother she did not feel frightened any more. However the backwash of this manoeuvre was that she was oppressed, when the storm had passed, by a sense of futility at not being herself, and a hatred of the person she had been (her mother) and herself for her self-duplicity.
Once she had become aware of this false way of overcoming the anxiety she was exposed to when she was herself, had to decide whether avoiding experiencing such anxiety, by avoiding being herself, was a cure worse than her disease.


The embodied and unembodied self - David

He had grown up taking entire for granted that what he called his ‘self’ and his ‘personality’ were two quite separate things.
His ideal was, never to give himself away to others.
The central split is between his ‘own’ self and his ‘personality’.
Such a schizoid individual in one sense is trying to be omnipotent by enclosing within his own being, without recourse to a creative relationship with others, modes of relationship that require the effective presence to him of other people and of the outer world. The imagined advantages are safety for the true self, isolation and hence freedom from others, self-sufficiency, and control.
The disadvantages are that this project is impossible and being a false hope, leads on to persistent despair. Secondly, a persistent, haunting sense of futility is the equally inevitable outcome, since the hidden shut-up self, in disowning participation in the quasi-autonomous activities of the false-self, is living only mentally. Moreover, this shut-up self, being isolated, is unable to be enriched by outer experience, and so the whole inner world comes to be more and more impoverished, until the individual may come to feel he is merely a vacuum.
The world as it is, and other people as they are, are the dangers. The self then seeks by being unembodied to transcend the world and hence to be safe. The constant dread of all that is there, of being overwhelmed, is potentiated rather than mitigated by the need to keep the world at bay. Yet the self may at the same time long more than anything for participation in the world. Thus, its greatest longing is felt as it greatest weakness and giving in to this weakness is its greatest dread, since in participation the individual fears that his vacuum will be obliterated, that he will be engulfed or otherwise lose his identity, which has come to be equated with the transcendence in a void.
If the individual delegates all transactions between himself and the other to a system within his being which is not ‘him’, then the world is experienced to be unreal and all that belongs to this system is felt to be false, futile and meaningless. Instead of (Self/body) <> other, the situation is self <> (body/other).

The self, is precluded from having a direct relationship with real things and real people


His act is always the product of a false self. The act or the deed is never his true reality. He wishes to remain perpetually uncommitted ‘to the objective element’ - hence the deed is always (or at least he believes it to be) a pretended, a supposed performance, and he may actively cultivate as far as he can that ‘inner’ negation of all that he does in an effort to declare everything that he does ‘null and void’ so that in the world, in reality, in ‘the objective element’, nothing of ‘him’ shall exist, and no footprints or fingerprints of the ‘self’ shall have been left.
The discussion of the possibility of friendship gets stuck at the dilemma: if two beings are not ‘wanting’ in anything, why should they want anything from the other? It is on this central issue that the schizoid person’s life is liable to founder - is he self-sufficient or does he ‘want’ anything?
James in some respects ‘took after’ his father. One of his father’s irritating characteristics was a way of asking people at the table if they had had enough to eat, and a tendency to press them to have more, even when they had said quite clearly that they were satisfied. James ‘took after’ his father in this respect: he always made a point of politely inquiring this of guests at the table. At first it appeared as no more than a generous concern that others should have enough. But his solicitations then came to be compulsive and to be carried past all tolerable bounds, so that he made himself a complete nuisance and occasioned general embarrassment. In this, he took up what he sensed were the aggressive implications in his father’s actions, and exposed these implications, through exaggerating them in his own adaptation, to general ridicule and anger. He, in fact, evoked from others the feelings he had towards his father but was unable to express directly to his face. Instead, he produced what amounted to a satirical comment on his father through the medium of a compulsive caricature of him.

title: Never Let Me Go
text:
Key Themes

Core Theme: The “Concertinaed” Life (Mortality on Fast-Forward)
This is the heart of the novel. By shortening the characters’ lifespans to ~30 years, Ishiguro magnifies the human condition.

The Universal Truth: We are all “terminal.” Whether we live to 30 or 80, the result is the same. The clones are not monsters; they are mirrors.
The Tragic Irony: despite knowing their time is short, the clones waste precious years on petty jealousy, social posturing, and “fitting in”—just like regular humans who waste time on office politics despite knowing life is short.
The Lesson: We often wait until the very end (the “completion”) to prioritize what actually matters: love and connection.



Secondary Key Themes
1. Willful Ignorance (The “Shadows”)

The novel critiques how society handles uncomfortable truths. The public knows the organ program exists, but they keep it in the “shadows” so they don’t have to feel guilty.
Real-world parallel: We enjoy the benefits of unethical labor (e.g., fast fashion, cheap electronics) by keeping the workers out of sight and out of mind.

2. The Myth of “Proving Your Soul”

The Hope: Kathy and Tommy believe that if they can prove they have souls (through art), the system will save them.
The Reality: The system knows they have souls; it just doesn’t care. Efficiency and survival (curing cancer) trump morality.
Key takeaway: Being “human” and “worthy” is not enough to save you from a system designed to exploit you.

3. Institutionalization & Conformity

The “Invisible Fence”: The reason the clones don’t run away is psychological, not physical. They have been indoctrinated to believe their only value is in dying for others.
Learned Helplessness: They fear the unknown world outside more than they fear certain death. This mirrors how people stay in abusive relationships or oppressive jobs because it is “what they know.”

4. Memory as Resistance

In the end, Kathy loses everything—her friends, her love, her future.
However, she refuses to “let go” of her memories. Narrating the story is her act of defiance. The system can take her kidney and her heart, but it cannot take the memory of Hailsham.



Summary Table: The Clones vs. Us








Theme
The Clones’ Experience
The Human Experience (Metaphor)




Life Purpose
“Donating” organs to others.
Giving our labor/bodies to the economy/society.


Deferrals
Believing art/love can delay death.
Believing success/money/legacy can make us immortal.


The End
“Completing” (Dying young).
Dying (Eventually).


Distraction
Obsessing over collections/rumors.
Obsessing over career/status/material goods.





Final Thought
The book asks a haunting question: If you knew exactly when you were going to die, would you live your life differently? The tragedy of the clones is that even though they did know, they largely lived the same way we do: distracted, hopeful for a miracle, and compliant until the end.

title: Never Let Me Go
text:
Thoughts on the Book
I thought it was quite an interesting book, where the focus is more on the individuals that would be impacted if cloning were to be allowed, instead of the societal focus that i think would be more the pov for other sci-fi books. But I think there is an excessive amount of focus. I would think the aim of such a focus or such a pov is to draw or allude to the fact that the students have a soul, despite them being just clones and despite the society wanting them to stay in the shadows. But I think from the POV of the reader its abit warped, because we start the story with them as people, as normal students and we are not given the knowledge of them being clones and the author, i feel, does a very irritating and repetitive thing of making references to things that we have no knowledge of, I think it is to mirror how the Hailsham students feel at the cottages or how the students feel when they were at Hailsham, were everything was really hush hush and kept under wraps, but the effect is not ideal. This is contrasted to something like the god of small things, where the initial death of Sophie Mol is already presented at the start and the rest of the book is watching the train wreck unfold, showing that there is no need to withold information from the reader in order to have an interesting book or an interesting plot.
I feel the emotions of the characters also don’t really make sense at times, much like one of the reflection questions where i mean why don’t the clones just think of escaping and why are they operating within the confines of their system? But yeah maybe its because they are clones and they have been programmed for this. But the issue is just that some parts of the story just don’t really make sense in the context of a normal society, in that how come the clones don’t all just operate in a factory and then who would be owning these clones and why they would be threatened or disgusted by clones? Also, it doesnt really make sense that one would be disgusted of the clones, i mean for the reader, because to the reader they have been portrayed as basically human so the reader is unable to empathise with the rest of the population in the book which states that the general population is disgusted. And the scandal in the book where the scientist is able to create babies that are genetically advanced, it makes it seem like the scientist is creating a new species and they are getting disgusted, but tbh they are just humans as well, just that they are clones? And there isn’t really the same uncanny valley effect I feel?
And what is the point of allowing the clones to interact with normal people and letting them grow up in a normal circumstance? And what is the point of creating them in different images? Why wouldn’t they just use one person with ideal genes up to that point and is super healthy? I think the novel leaves alot of questions unanswered and seems to focus on strange things like sex between the clones, I don’t think it does a very good job at this, it leaves it quite awkward and it also doesnt really flesh out how the protagonist and Tommy were in love, just that they felt for each other and that was about it. And so the novel is kinda not here and not there, building up to a revelation that I think most would have seem coming (somewhat) and the rest of the book is just the interpersonal relationships between clones that is not really that interesting….

title: Never Let Me Go
text:
Reading Reflections

Introduction
A thirty-one-year-old woman named Kathy narrates this haunting tale, drawing the reader gradually into her recollections of her life at Hailsham, the idyllic boarding school where she grew up. She and her best friends, Ruth and Tommy, were encouraged by their teachers to create works of art from an early age, to collect cherished objects, and to take good care of their health. There are no parents in their world, only a handful of teachers, some of whom seem to be deeply troubled by their position at the school. Kathy’s friend Ruth is bossy and manipulative, while Kathy herself is gentle and self-contained. Both are drawn to Tommy, a boy given to explosive fits of temper. What is revealed, as Kathy’s reminiscences accumulate, is a life of preparation for a special role in a world that has begun to exploit the medical possibilities of genetic technology. A tale of deceptive simplicity, Never Let Me Go slowly reveals an extraordinary emotional depth and resonance—and takes its place among Kazuo Ishiguro’s finest work.

title: Never Let Me Go
text:
Questions and Topics for Discussion

Kathy introduces herself as an experienced carer. She prides herself on knowing how to keep her donors calm, “even before fourth donation” [p. 3]. How long does it take for the meaning of such terms as “donation,” “carer,” and “completed” to be fully revealed?

I think it was revealed quite early, that they were just being used for their organs, but it was not immediately obvious what they were, whether they were robots or clones or something like that. I rmb quite early on there was mention that they were to be donors and it would be quite easy to surmise that they were being used for their organs. The part that was harder to decipher, but with no real meaning, is the role of the higher ups, of Madame and of Miss Lucy and how they were all pawns in the giant chess game and how nobody really has any power in the book and they are all just a part of the process and just watching things happen, powerless to stop them.

Kathy addresses us directly, with statements like “I don’t know how it was where you were, but at Hailsham we used to have some form of medical every week” [p. 13], and she thinks that we too might envy her having been at Hailsham [p. 4]. What does Kathy assume about anyone she might be addressing, and why?

I didn’t really like this fourth wall break, because it detracts from the narrative of the story, initially it seems like we are just in Kathy’s head, but then this breaks out of this narrative to address us and to me its an awkward attempt at trying to bring the reader into the story and to me it fell flat, because it assumes that the reader is a clone as well, but then it makes it seem like we are reading a journal entry of sorts of Kathy, but this is not the case and so its abit strange.

Why is it important for Kathy to seek out donors who are “from the past,” “people from Hailsham” [p. 5]? She learns from a donor who’d grown up at an awful place in Dorset that she and her friends at Hailsham had been really “lucky” [p. 6]. How does the irony of this designation grow as the novel goes on? What does Hailsham represent for Kathy, and why does she say at the end that Hailsham is “something no one can take away” [p. 287]?

I assume donors from Hailsham are clones that Kathy can empatise with and who she can relate to. Compared to the rest of the clones, Kathy alienates them and doesn’t really interact with them, only using them for sex? Which idk what is the point of that plot point? That the clones have urges too? I don’t get the irony though, if you could explain that to me that would be great? I thought that it was great that they were from Hailsham, given that they were clones. I mean if the irony is that even though they were at Hailsham there is no difference because they are clones and have to die to donate their organs then i think its’ quite a silly irony, because like Miss Lucy, I agree that its not in vain, I would much rather spend 18 years in luxury than 18 year in slavery, if at the end of the day we were to die and be farmed for our organs anyways. This is something like the idea that if i were to die in a car crash and have my organs harvested, was the life that I lived before that pointless? If I am to know that I will die at 30, will the life that I live before that pointless? How about if i live to be 80? I guess my point is that the clones just have had a shortened lifespan, they live to be 20 ish i guess, and within that span, they get to experience culture, to read to love to live, and the novel points at the idea that what is the point of it all and is there even a point and I would venture to say that if the clones didn’t have a point and if Hailsham didn’t have a point then life as we know it wouldn’t have a point. And I think this would have been a good point that the author could have ventured into showing, that even for the clones, knowing that they were to die and knowing the way they were going to die, that they were still able to live a very full life, instead of focusing on teenage interactions and teenage angst?? Please explain to me if I am missing the point of the author. Hailsham represents childhood for Kathy and I assume for the rest of the clones as well. Hailsham is something no one can take away, because they take away the physical portions of the clones, their organs but their souls, which they cultivated at Hailsham, is something that they will never be able to take away.

Kathy tells the reader, “How you were regarded at Hailsham, how much you were liked and respected, had to do with how good you were at ‘creating’” [p. 16]. What were Hailsham’s administrators trying to achieve in attaching a high value to creativity?

They were trying to prove to the wider society that the clones had a soul, that they were able to create art, that they were able to reflect the soul within and that they possessed what it meant to be human. That creativity was something that only a human could accomplish and in doing so and in making the art pieces and displaying them in the galleries, that the clones too were human.

Kathy’s narration is the key to the novel’s disquieting effect. First person narration establishes a kind of intimacy between narrator and reader. What is it like having direct access to Kathy’s mind and feelings? How would the novel be different if narrated from Tommy’s point of view, or Ruth’s, or Miss Emily’s?

I think it provides some level of intimacy but i think it falls short in illuminating the main themes of the book. There is alot of focus on the drama between the clones and it really just seems like petty teenager drama, which really makes no sense and only at the end is there more focus on the ethical issues of cloning. But yeah I think the novel would be very different, because all the characters have different focuses on what they wanted to accomplish during their time at hailsham, Tommy was filled with rage and had so much concerns about his art and how he was unable to produce the art that he was required to and which was held in such high regard at hailsham. Ruth was obsessed with fitting in and performing the correct societal decisions to fit in. Miss Emily’s pov would have been alot more illuminating and with the worries about how to let the clones flourish and how to convince the rest of the society that the clones were living. But this touches on one point that really leaves a sour taste in my mouth. Emily and Madame were both people that committed, I guess, their entire lives to making sure that the clones were able to be perceived as human, even going into debt to try and keep Hailsham going. But near the end of the book, when the things, the clones that they were trying to save come to them asking for answer and asking for help, Emily left to take care of a cabinet and Madame barely want to interact with them. I think its incongruent with what I would have expected of someone who so passionately wants to help the clones and there is also no information on my Emily and Madame are living together, they gay or just really good friends, I mean doesn’t really matter to the plot of the story but its just a loose end that isn’t really tied up and I don’t like it. It seems like the story and the revelation at the end was just a revelation for revelation’s sake and the suspense is really artificial, its not like Emily was a clone as well or that she had a child that was a clone or something like that, Emily is just shown to be the one that is trying to help them with no reason and little depth to the characters. They dont feel real. Contrast this with John Steinbeck’s writing, say for East of Eden or Grapes or Wrath where every character, no matter how small, feels real, has a role to play, has needs, passions, goals, intentions. The characters in this book just feel very superficial.

What are some of Ruth’s most striking character traits? How might her social behavior, at Hailsham and later at the Cottages, be explained? Why does she seek her “possible” so earnestly [pp. 159–67]?

Ruth most striking character traits are that she seeks constantly to conform and to please those in power or those perceived to be in power. I guess it was genetically encoded within them? As clones society wouldn’t want them to revolt and to escape so they made them as conformist as possible? She seeks her possible because she wants to believe that she could have another life and that it could be different? I’m not sure on this point.

One of the most notable aspects of life at Hailsham is the power of the group. Students watch each other carefully and try on different poses, attitudes, and ways of speaking. Is this behavior typical of most adolescents, or is there something different about the way the students at Hailsham seek to conform?

I think its because they are not socialised to the wider society and they have to learn from each other or form media like sitcoms and they are alienated or they are alienating themselves, somehow having an understanding that they are different from the rest of the population, even when they are in the town they don’t really interact with the rest of the population in the city and they mostly go about their own way, doing their own thing. The only real thing that they get to do is to interact with the shopkeeper that was telling them about the art and this is where they feel they can connect with the shopkeeper since this is something that they have practiced and this is something that they feel they have domain knowledge in and are able to converse about. I think this behaviour would be typical of most adolscents, I think, at least in my mind that the clones are no different from humans, after all why would they be, if they have organs that are functioning and are perfectly able to communicate and to speak to others? The students at Hailsham seek to conform with one another and to maintain the societal structure that they have built within Hailsham and I guess this is quite a normal thing for teens to do? I feel like there is a metaphor in here but I don’t really see it.

How do Madame and Miss Emily react to Kathy and Tommy when they come to request a deferral? Defending her work at Hailsham, Miss Emily says, “Look at you both now! You’ve had good lives, you’re educated and cultured” [p. 261]. What is revealed in this extended conversation, and how do these revelations affect your experience of the story?

It is revealed that Emily and Madame didn’t really have any power in the impact of their lives and they were just facilitating the development of the clones, with no real power on how they are able to defer or to prevent the donations. I think this revelation makes me think back to the purpose of the artworks and the purpose of the learnings at Hailsham, but I think i might be missing something here as well, please fill me in as to what you think should be the case here.

Why does Tommy draw animals? Why does he continue to work on them even after he learns that there will be no deferral?

No clue why he draws animals, maybe he feels the animalistic urge of anger and the primal nature of man??? But he continues to work on them because Tommy never did them for the sake of the gallery or for Madame, he did art because he enjoyed making art, he enjoyed creating and he had a soul. Again, i might be missing some point here so please fill me in.

Kathy reminds Madame of the scene in which Madame watched her dancing to a song on her Judy Bridgewater tape. How is Kathy’s interpretation of this event different from Madame’s? How else might it be interpreted? Is the song’s title again recalled by the book’s final pages [pp. 286–88]?

Yeah i don’t really get the book’s title and the songs title.. who is letting who go? Society letting the shadow people go? I think this entire book is just a very drawn out concept of the Ones who walked away from Omelas and that book was just really succinct because at the end of the day its quite a shallow concept and we all understand it, put be all try to avoid the understanding of it. We all know that our conveniences, our buildings, construction, services, cheap goods, fast fashion, is built upon the pollution and the labour of those in poor countries slogging away and breathing the fumes into their lungs but yeah we don’t really care and thats all there is to it, I think this book draws it out too much or at least doesn’t really bring to the surface any super interesting things or insights. I also don’t get the point of the different interpretations of the event, Kathy’s interpretation I guess is meant to be sad but imo it evicts little to no emotion in the reader because its kinda incongruent with the character and how they never mention that they want to have kids or anything like that. But i guess it shows how each character and everyone in society just has their own narrative for a specific incident and different morals and views as a result.

After their visit to Miss Emily and Madame, Kathy tells Tommy that his fits of rage might be explained by the fact that “at some level you always knew” [p. 275]. Does this imply that Kathy didn’t? Does it imply that Tommy is more perceptive than Kathy?

Yeah i guess it does imply that Kathy didn’t know, but I don’t think it reflects that Tommy is more perceptive, given that Tommy is shows to be quite daft, but it might show that Tommy see’s the bigger picture, and doesn’t focus on the smaller details and intricacies of life, only learning to do so later in life in his drawings where they are much more intricate.

Does the novel examine the possibility of human cloning as a legitimate question for medical ethics, or does it demonstrate that the human costs of cloning are morally repellent, and therefore impossible for science to pursue? What kind of moral and emotional responses does the novel provoke? If you extend the scope of the book’s critique, what are its implications for our own society?

Yeah i don’t think the book covered this portion very well, as a result of the focus on the relationships of the clones I guess… but feel free to add on anything that I am missing.

The novel takes place in “the late 1990s,” and a postwar science boom has resulted in human cloning and the surgical harvesting of organs to cure cancer and other diseases. In an interview with January Magazine Ishiguro said that he is not interested in realism.* In spite of the novel’s fictitious premise, however, how “realistically” does Never Let Me Go reflect the world we live in, where scientific advancement can be seemingly irresistible?

Help me come up with an answer for this question

The teacher Lucy Wainright wanted to make the children more aware of the future that awaited them. Miss Emily believed that in hiding the truth, “We were able to give you something, something which even now no one will ever take from you, and we were able to do that principally by sheltering you. . . . Sometimes that meant we kept things from you, lied to you. . . . But . . . we gave you your childhoods” [p. 268]. In the context of the story as a whole, is this a valid argument?

Help me come up with an answer for this question

Is it surprising that Miss Emily admits feeling revulsion for the children at Hailsham? Does this indicate that she believes Kathy and Tommy are not fully human? What is the nature of the moral quandary Miss Emily and Madame have gotten themselves into?

Help me come up with an answer for this question

Critic Frank Kermode has noted that “Ishiguro is fundamentally a tragic novelist; there is always a disaster, remote but urgent, imagined but real, at the heart of his stories” [London Review of Books, April 21, 2005]. How would you describe the tragedy at the heart of Never Let Me Go?

Help me come up with an answer for this question

Some reviewers have expressed surprise that Kathy, Tommy, and their friends never try to escape their ultimate fate. They cling to the possibility of deferral, but never attempt to vanish into the world of freedom that they view from a distance. Yet they love the film The Great Escape, “the moment the American jumps over the barbed wire on his bike” [p. 99]. Why might Ishiguro have chosen to present them as fully resigned to their early deaths?

Help me come up with an answer for this question

Reread the novel’s final paragraph, in which Kathy describes a flat, windswept field with a barbed wire fence “where all sorts of rubbish had caught and tangled.” She imagines Tommy appearing here in “the spot where everything I’d ever lost since my childhood had washed up” [p. 287]. What does the final sentence indicate about Kathy’s state of mind as she faces her losses and her own death—stoicism, denial, courage, resolution?

Help me come up with an answer for this question

In a recent interview, Ishiguro talked about Never Let Me Go: “There are things I am more interested in than the clone thing. How are they trying to find their place in the world and make sense of their lives? To what extent can they transcend their fate? As time starts to run out, what are the things that really matter? Most of the things that concern them concern us all, but with them it is concertinaed into this relatively short period of time. These are things that really interest me and, having come to the realization that I probably have limited opportunities to explore these things, that’s what I want to concentrate on. I can see the appeal of travel books and journalism and all the rest of it and I hope there will be time to do them all one day. But I just don’t think that day is now.” How do these remarks relate to your own ideas about the book? [Interview with Nicholas Wroe, The Guardian, February 2, 2005.]

Help me come up with an answer for this question

title: Never Let Me Go
text:
Thoughts from Gemini

Part 1: General Feedback on Your Critique
1. The “Boring” Focus vs. Sci-Fi Plot - Your thought: You felt the book focused too much on teenage relationships and withholding information, rather than the societal mechanics of cloning. - Analysis: You are right that this is frustrating if you expect traditional sci-fi. However, Ishiguro is writing Speculative Fiction focused on the human condition. - The Counter-Point: The author doesn’t care about how the cloning works; he cares about how we deal with mortality. The clones are a metaphor for all humans. We all know we are going to die (complete), yet we distract ourselves with petty interpersonal drama, love triangles, and art. The “boring” parts are the point: even in the face of doom, humans prioritize relationships.
2. Why don’t they escape? (The “Logic” Gap) - Your thought: It makes no sense that they don’t run away or that they aren’t kept in a factory. - The Missing Piece: This is the most chilling part of the book, often called hegemony or institutionalization. - Explanation: Think of an elephant tied to a stake with a thin rope. It could break the rope, but it learned as a baby it couldn’t, so it stops trying. The clones are indoctrinated. They are told their “purpose” is to donate. They don’t run away because they don’t believe they deserve to survive. They believe the system is inevitable. This mirrors how real humans accept unjust systems (capitalism, caste systems, slavery) because “that’s just how the world is.”
3. The “Uncanny Valley” and Disgust - Your thought: It doesn’t make sense that normal people are disgusted by them if they look human. - The Nuance: The disgust isn’t physical; it is moral guilt. - Explanation: Society knows they are murdering these “students” for organs. To live with that guilt, society must view them as soulless monsters (like spiders, as Madame says). If they admitted the clones were just like them, they would have to stop the organ harvesting (and let their own children die of cancer). The disgust is a defense mechanism.


Part 2: Feedback on Your Discussion Answers
Here are pointers on specific questions where you asked for clarification or felt the book was lacking. #### Q2: The “Fourth Wall” Break - Your Analysis: You felt it was awkward and fell flat because it assumes the reader is a clone. - Pointer: You are technically correct—Kathy is addressing a specific audience, but she is not addressing 21st-century human readers. She is addressing other clones in her world. - When she says, “I don’t know how it was where you were,” she is speaking to other Carers/Donors from lesser institutions (not Hailsham). This emphasizes her snobbery and isolation. She isn’t breaking the fourth wall to talk to us; we are eavesdropping on a conversation between clones.

Q3: The Irony of “Lucky” (You asked for an explanation here)

Your Question: I don’t get the irony though… I would much rather spend 18 years in luxury…
The Analysis: You made a great point about “Is a short life pointless?” However, here is the irony you might be missing:

The Cruelty of Hope: Hailsham educated them, gave them art, literature, and complex emotions (souls). By doing so, it made their fate much more painful.
If they were raised in a factory like battery hens, ignorant of art and love, their deaths would be sad but perhaps they wouldn’t suffer psychologically.
The Irony: Hailsham “saved” them from a bad childhood, but by making them fully human, it condemned them to a torture that factory-clones wouldn’t feel: the torture of knowing what they are losing. They were given the capacity to dream, only to have those dreams crushed.




Q5: Miss Emily and Madame’s Reaction

Your Analysis: You felt their dismissal of Tommy and Kathy at the end was “incongruent” and “superficial” compared to Steinbeck’s characters.
Pointer: This is actually a profound critique of performative activism.

Madame and Miss Emily dedicated their lives to “helping” clones, but they were still disgusted by them. (Recall the scene where Madame flinches when they walk past her).
Ishiguro is showing that even “good” people who fight for justice can still harbor deep prejudice. They fought for the idea of the clones, but they didn’t actually want to interact with the reality of them.
Why did they give up? Because society didn’t care. Science (The Morningdale Scandal) advanced too fast. People wanted superior organs/babies more than they wanted ethics. Miss Emily is bitter because she lost the war against convenience.




Q6: Ruth’s “Possible”

Your Analysis: She seeks conformity; maybe she is genetically encoded?
Pointer: It’s less about genetics and more about denial.

Ruth looks for her “possible” (the human she was cloned from) in an office building because she desperately wants to believe she comes from “proper” people.
The tragedy is revealed when they realize they are likely cloned from “trash”—prostitutes, junkies, the homeless. Ruth’s search is a desperate attempt to prove she has a respectable lineage, which ultimately fails.





Summary of Your Reflection
Strengths: - You accurately identified the book’s pacing and structural quirks (the “hush hush” nature). - Your comparison to God of Small Things is excellent—Roy reveals the tragedy early to focus on the how, whereas Ishiguro hides it to focus on the denial. - You correctly identified that the characters feel powerless (pawns in a game).
Areas for Deeper Consideration: - Shift your lens from Literal to Metaphorical: The book fails as a logical sci-fi manual (factory farming makes more sense economically). It succeeds as a metaphor for how humans ignore the suffering of others (sweatshops, slaughterhouses) to enjoy a comfortable life.

The “Pointless” Life: You asked, “If the clones didn’t have a point… then life as we know it wouldn’t have a point.” That is exactly the author’s point. We are all Kathy. We are all going to “complete.” The tragedy is not that they die young; the tragedy is that they (and we) accept our fate so passively.

Together, the research reveals the psychological assets that mature paragons of grit have in common. There are four. They counter each of the buzz-killers listed above, and they tend to develop, over the years, in a particular order. First comes interest. Passion begins with intrinsically enjoying what you do. Every gritty person I’ve studied can point to aspects of their work they enjoy less than others, and most have to put up with at least one or two chores they don’t enjoy at all. Nevertheless, they’re captivated by the endeavor as a whole. title: East of Eden
text:
Summary
Yeah book was really good and sometimes its was a hard read because as a third party you know what is going to happen and its like watching a train wreck but overall the book was really good. Good, realistic characters with good development and character arcs. Was really frustrating seeing Adam make bad decisions again and again but I guess that is just how life works, from Cathy to the Cabbage to his children, it seems he’s always floating and never really there but its an interesting element. And Steinbeck has this mastery over the side characters and how he gives them time to breathe and full form, like how he gave Joe his own mind and actions when in reality the purpose of Joe was to make sure that the will saw the light of day and in performing this one purpose, he was given a mind to scheme against Cathy and his anxiety and his confidence and his hubris and at the end of all that, his sudden and uneventful death.
Steinbeck really excels at the silence, the side-plots parallel to the main plot and he does it in a way where the reader (me) doesnt yearn to get back to the main plot, and I dont even realise at times that I am even in the side plot, it genuinely just flows.
Anyway so maybe a short summary, started with the Antangonist, which I would say is Cathy and how she is note! Not Evil! but rather just different, which is an interesting idea, how perhaps some people are just born with the propensity to be mean and to be evil and this is not something that they realise offhand but through interactions with others, they realise that they lack a certain something, a lack of capacity to give and receive love and this leaves them feeling empty or hollow.
Anyways so she gets beaten up and she goes to Adam and UHHHH idk his brother. And his brother is able to sense the same thing as Cathy. OH I think essentially Cathy is the Devil LOL ok i need to insert the story of Cain and Abel because that is essentially the story repeated twice, and how people have the TIMSHEL - ‘thou mayest’ symbolizing the power of free will and choice, humans have the choice to overcome their circumstances or “rule over sin”. This is in direct contrast to Cathy and how she resigned herself to her own fate of being “evil” and lacking the basic faculties for love. We see this manifest in Charles, with his murderous nature, and in Cal, in his inability to behave as Aron has. We see Adam repeating the mistakes of his own father, by showing favoritism to Aron over Cal, and in doing so, inadvertently perpetuating the mistakes of his father. But one interesting thing to note, rather than the silent mother that only acts as a bystander to the damage, Lee, takes action and illustrates Timshel, making Adam forgive Cal and in doing so, undoes the damage or at the very least mitigates the damage done by Adam.
But yes ok so a broad summary of the story: 1. Introduction of Cathy and how evil she is 2. Introduction of Samuel Hamilton and the Hamilton family and shows how even in poverty, a family can be rich, juxtaposed to the Trask family with somewhat the inverse 3. Cathy interacting with Trask family 4. Adam gets shot and children are abandoned 5. Cathy gets rich and displays just how evil and scheming she is 6. Samuel forces Adam to name his children, shaking him out of his slumber 7. Children meet Abra 8. Aron dreams of becoming a pastor, he bends the world to fit in his worldview 9. Cal semi-resigns to his fate of being an evil person 10. Aron dies at war after leaving and learning his mother is a whore 11. Cal needs to reconcile his sense of self and seek approval from his father
But yeah there are so many themes in the book that its hard to manually list them down so I might get Gemini to provide me with more but just to name a few:

Nature vs Nurture

Whether it is possible to break free from the chains with Timshel and the ability to change one’s life
At the end it is revealed that even Cathy, in all her evilness, retains some sense of childishness, clinging on to the concept of an Alice, and how even Cathy knows that she is not all powerful and she sometimes wishes, when times are hard, that she can become small and hidden and out of view of people.
Adam vs Charles Trask - whether either or them was just born this way and of a better temperment than the other or whether this was imbued into them by their father through his strict regimentation and favouritism



On the effect and impact of parenthood and the perception of the parent as God

Steinbeck draws parallels heavily to the concepts of gifts or offerings from the children to their parents, much like the offerings that Cain and Abel made to God. And just like Cain and Abel, some offerings are much more favored and some not. A puppy was favored, even when it was really low effort, at least in Charles POV but (a gold watch??) that Charles gave? I forgot, was not regarded at all.
Charles’s gift: You couldn’t remember Charles’s gift to Cyrus. It was a high-quality, expensive German clasp knife that he had saved up for a long time to buy. This makes the rejection even more painful, as Adam’s gift (a stray puppy he just found) was low-effort but received with far more love, reinforcing the theme of arbitrary parental favor.
Similarly, fifteen thousand dollars to cover the Cabbage mistake. A mistake that greatly shaped Cal and Aron’s childhood, was not only not regarded, but shunned.
Why did God shun Cain’s offering? Did god cause the murder of Cain, or is it Timshel, the ability to do what one wants, and if one wants to murder, not even god can stop you.



The Land as Character
The Salinas Valley isn’t just a setting; it’s a symbolic landscape.

A New Eden: It’s the “East of Eden,” the land of Nod where Cain was exiled. It’s not the original paradise, but a new, harsher land (full of dust and drought) where humanity must choose to be good.
Wealth vs. Poverty: The land reflects the characters’ fortunes. The Hamiltons are “rich” in spirit, love, and ingenuity (Samuel can find water), but they are dirt-poor and can barely make a living from their dry, stubborn patch of land. The Trasks are “poor” in spirit but sit on a (stolen) fortune and one of the most fertile pieces of land in the valley.
Adam’s Failure: Adam’s grand failure is the cabbage shipment. He tries to conquer the land with technology and business (refrigeration) to make a fortune, but he fails spectacularly. This contrasts with Samuel, who works with the land (digging wells) and lives a life of meaning, not riches.



The Power of Names
Steinbeck is extremely deliberate with his names, particularly the “A” and “C” parallel.

Cain & Abel: The first generation is Charles and Adam. The second is Cal and Aron.
Breaking the Cycle: The pattern suggests a fated repeat of the biblical tragedy. However, the book introduces Abra. She starts as Aron’s idealized, pure love, but she grows into a real, flawed, and compassionate woman. Her name starts with “A,” but she ultimately breaks the pattern by rejecting the “good” (and hollow) Aron and choosing the “dark” (and real) Cal. She represents the new path forward.



Scars and Physical Marks
Many characters are physically marked by their internal sin or struggles.

Charles: Gets a dark scar on his forehead from straining to move a boulder for his father (another rejected “gift”). This mark is a clear parallel to the “Mark of Cain.”
Cathy/Kate: As she ages, her hands become gnarled and crippled with arthritis. Her physical form twists to match her internal, monstrous nature.
Adam: Receives a scar in the army, and later, the “shot” from Cathy is the pivotal wound that sends him into a decade-long emotional coma.



Wealth and Inheritance
The “sins of the father” are passed down not just emotionally but financially.

The Trask fortune is built on a lie—Cyrus likely stole it during his time in the army. This “poisoned” inheritance taints everything it touches. Adam tries to lose it, and Cal doubles it through cynical war profiteering (selling beans).
The Hamilton inheritance is the opposite. They have no money, but Samuel passes down wisdom, stories, and a moral compass. Lee, who is not related by blood, is the one who truly inherits Samuel’s philosophical legacy and passes it on to Cal.



1. The Role of the Narrator (Steinbeck Himself)
You noted the side plots, but the most significant “side plot” is the entire Hamilton family. Why are they there?

The “I” Voice: Steinbeck inserts himself as the narrator, “I.” He is the grandson of Samuel Hamilton. This is a deliberate choice to break the fictional barrier. The novel isn’t just a story; it’s a personal testament.
The Hamiltons as the “Human” Baseline: The Trask family is mythic. They are a biblical allegory, larger-than-life, and live in the shadow of a divine curse. The Hamiltons, by contrast, are human. They are poor, clever, flawed, loving, and real.
The Bridge: Samuel Hamilton acts as the bridge between these two worlds. He is the only one who can talk to the mythic, “floating” Adam Trask. He is the one who names the twins (like the biblical Adam), gives Adam a conscience, and forces him back into the human race.
Deeper Meaning: By placing his own family into the story, Steinbeck is saying this isn’t just an ancient myth. The struggle of Cain and Abel, of good and evil, of Timshel, is not just a story. It is his story, our story, and it is happening right now in the very real, dusty soil of America.



2. Lee: The Moral and Intellectual Core
You identified Lee as the one who “takes action,” which is perfect. But his role is even more profound. He is arguably the book’s true hero and its philosophical engine.

The Mask of Pidgin English: Lee’s greatest insight is his explanation for why he speaks pidgin. It’s not ignorance; it’s a weapon and a shield. He explains that people need him to fit their stereotype of a Chinese servant, so he plays the part to be left alone and to manage their expectations. This is a sharp social commentary on race and identity. He is the most intelligent, educated, and articulate character in the book, and he chooses to hide it.
The True Parent: Adam is the biological father, but Lee is the true parent. He raises Cal and Aron. He cooks, cleans, educates, and provides the moral and philosophical guidance (like the Timshel story) that Adam is too lost to give. He is the novel’s true father figure.
The Engine of Timshel: Lee isn’t just the one who explains Timshel. He researched it. He spent years with his relatives (the “four old men”) in San Francisco, studying the Hebrew text of Genesis to find the exact meaning of the word. He is the book’s theologian. The entire novel’s central theme is a product of Lee’s intellectual and spiritual labor. He gives the book its meaning.



3. The Failure of “Goodness”: The Problem of Aron
You identified Cal’s struggle perfectly, but the flip side is Aron’s. Steinbeck isn’t just saying “it’s hard to be good.” He’s making a radical argument: naive, untested “goodness” is just as destructive as “evil.”

Hollow Idealism: Aron is the “Abel” figure, the one favored by his father. But his goodness is hollow. He cannot face reality. He needs his mother to be a saint in heaven and his girlfriend, Abra, to be a pure, virginal ideal.
Weakness, Not Virtue: When Cal forces him to face the truth (that their mother is a whore), Aron doesn’t struggle and overcome; he shatters. His “goodness” was just a fragile fantasy. He immediately escapes into the army, an act of self-destruction, and is killed.
The Role of Abra: Abra is the key. Her name is the feminine version of “Abel.” She starts as Aron’s idealized “pure girl,” but she grows. She admits to Cal that she’s not pure, that she has “bad thoughts,” and that she’s tired of pretending. She finds Aron’s pure goodness “cold” and “empty.” Her decision to choose the flawed, dark, and real Cal over the “good” but hollow Aron is one of the most important moments in the book. She breaks the cycle by choosing a complete human, not a fantasy.



4. Cathy/Kate: The “Crack” in the Monster
You’re right that Steinbeck presents Cathy as a “monster,” a psychic malformation. But your note about her “childishness” is the key to her entire character.

The Longing for Alice: You’re right, she clings to Alice in Wonderland. This isn’t just a quirk. Alice falls down a rabbit hole into a world of nonsense where the rules don’t apply, a world she can control. This is Cathy’s a-moral worldview.
The Final Act: Why does she kill herself? It’s not just fear of Cal. It happens after she sees a picture of Aron, who is described as looking angelic and innocent. She is confronted with the one thing she cannot understand or control: genuine, naive goodness.
The Will: Her final, most twisted act is leaving her entire fortune to Aron. This is her last attempt to destroy: to poison the “good” son with her corrupt money.
The “Crack”: But her suicide, her clinging to the Alice book, and her fear of the dark all point to one thing: she is not a complete monster. There is a tiny, terrified, undeveloped child inside. This reinforces Timshel. Even the most “evil” character in the novel is not 100% evil. This suggests that perhaps, somewhere, she also had a choice, but she spent her entire life refusing to make it. Her evil wasn’t a force; it was a vacuum, an absence of humanity.
